content.text: What an Azure Subscription Is

An Azure subscription is the fundamental unit of billing, access control, and resource quota in Azure. Every Azure resource — virtual machines, databases, storage accounts, networking — belongs to exactly one subscription. The subscription acts as four simultaneous boundaries:
Billing boundary:
all charges for resources in the subscription roll up to a single invoice. Multiple subscriptions generate multiple invoices, enabling per-team, per-project, or per-environment cost reporting.
Access control boundary:

RBAC role assignments at the subscription scope apply to all resource groups and resources in the subscription. This is the largest scope at which an organization typically delegates administrative access.
Policy boundary:
Azure Policy assignments at the subscription scope apply to all resources in the subscription, controlling what resources can be created, where, and with what configuration.
Quota boundary:

Azure service limits (VM vCPUs, storage accounts, public IP addresses) are tracked and enforced per subscription per region. If one team exhausts a quota in a subscription, only that subscription is affected.

attrs.alt: Azure subscription as four simultaneous boundaries: billing, access control, policy, and quota — with resource groups as containers beneath it

content.text: Subscription Offer Types
A subscription is associated with an offer type that determines how Azure is billed and what pricing applies:
Offer Type
Who uses it
Billing model
Commitment
Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)
Individuals, startups, small teams
Monthly invoice, credit card or bank transfer
None — cancel anytime
Enterprise Agreement (EA)
Large enterprises (500+ seats typically)
Upfront commitment (Azure Prepayment/Monetary Commitment)
1 or 3 year commitment; discounted rates
Microsoft Customer Agreement (MCA)
Mid-market and enterprise via web
Monthly invoice with billing profile; no upfront commitment required
None for PAYG tier; Reserved Instances sold separately
Cloud Solution Provider (CSP)
Customers buying through Microsoft partners
Partner bills the customer; partner manages the subscription
Varies by partner
Azure Free Account
New Azure users
Free credits for 30 days + 12 months of free services
None
Dev/Test (PAYG Dev/Test, EA Dev/Test)
Development and testing workloads
Discounted rates for dev/test environments; not for production SLA
None (PAYG) or EA commitment

The offer type affects pricing but not the technical behavior of the subscription. A VM in a PAYG subscription behaves identically to the same VM in an EA subscription — only the billing rate differs.
Subscription Design Patterns
How many subscriptions to create and how to partition workloads across them is one of the most consequential cloud architecture decisions. The two extreme approaches are:
One subscription per workload / environment

Each application or business unit gets its own Production, Non-Production, and Dev subscriptions. This maximizes isolation: a misconfigured resource in one subscription cannot affect resources in another, quota exhaustion in one does not affect others, and RBAC can be cleanly delegated to each team for their own subscription.
Shared subscriptions

Multiple applications share a subscription, separated only by resource group naming conventions. This minimizes subscription management overhead and simplifies billing for small organizations, but at the cost of blast radius: an accidental delete or misconfigured policy in a shared subscription can affect all teams using it.

Microsoft's Cloud Adoption Framework recommends the subscription-per-environment-per-workload model for large organizations, with the Platform management group containing shared service subscriptions (networking, identity, monitoring) that are separate from workload subscriptions.

# List all subscriptions in the tenant (requires elevated access)
az account list --all \
  --query "[].{name:name, id:id, state:state, tenantId:tenantId}" \
  --output table

# Switch active subscription context
az account set --subscription "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001"

# Show current subscription details
az account show \
  --query "{name:name, id:id, state:state, isDefault:isDefault, tenantId:tenantId}" \
  --output jsonc

# Rename a subscription (display name only; ID never changes)
az account subscription rename \
  --id "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001" \
  --name "MyApp-Production"

# Move a subscription to a different management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name "Corp-Prod" \
  --subscription "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001"
Subscription Quotas and Limits

Azure enforces soft and hard limits per subscription per region. Soft limits (quotas) are the default ceiling; they can be increased by submitting a support request. Hard limits are fixed and cannot be raised regardless of support request.
Resource
Default soft limit
Hard limit
Notes
vCPUs per region (standard VMs)
350 per family (can increase)
Each VM family (D, E, F, N...) has its own sub-limit within the total
Public IP addresses (Basic)
Can be increased
Standard Public IP has a separate quota
Storage accounts per region
250 (can be increased by support)
Per subscription per region
Resource groups per subscription
Hard limit; cannot be increased
Resources per resource group
800 per resource type
Hard limit per provider/type combination
Virtual networks per region
Can be increased
App Service plans per region
100 (per SKU family)

# Check current quota usage for vCPUs in a region
az vm list-usage \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[?contains(name.value, 'cores') || contains(name.value, 'vCPU')].{name:name.localizedValue, current:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# Check quota for a specific VM family
az vm list-usage \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[?name.value=='standardDSv3Family'].{name:name.localizedValue, used:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# Check storage account quota
az storage account list \
  --query "length(@)" \
  --output tsv

# Request a quota increase (opens a support ticket via CLI)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name "vCPU-quota-increase" \
  --title "Request vCPU quota increase in East US" \
  --description "Need to increase Standard_DSv3 quota from 350 to 1000 vCPUs in East US for production scaling." \
  --problem-classification "/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/quota_service_guid/problemClassifications/compute_vm_quota_guid" \
  --severity "moderate" \
  --contact-first-name "Admin" \
  --contact-last-name "User" \
  --contact-method "email" \
  --contact-email "admin@contoso.com"

Request quota increases before you need them. Quota increase approvals can take 24–72 hours. If you are planning a large-scale deployment or a scaling event, submit the quota request at least a week in advance.
Subscription-Level Cost Management

Each subscription is a billing scope — the smallest scope at which you receive a separate Azure invoice. Within a subscription, Azure Cost Management lets you view spending by resource group, resource, service type, or tag. Setting budgets and cost alerts at the subscription level is the standard way to detect unexpected spending before it becomes a large bill.

# Set a monthly budget alert for a subscription
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "monthly-budget" \
  --amount 5000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date "2024-01-01" \
  --end-date "2025-01-01" \
  --resource-group-filter "" \
  --notifications '[
    {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 80,
      "contactEmails": ["admin@contoso.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Actual"
    },
    {
      "enabled": true,
      "operator": "GreaterThan",
      "threshold": 100,
      "contactEmails": ["admin@contoso.com", "finance@contoso.com"],
      "thresholdType": "Forecasted"
    }
  ]'

# View cost breakdown by service in the current subscription for the last 30 days
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2024-01-31 \
  --query "[].{service:instanceData, cost:pretaxCost, currency:currency}" \
  --output table

# View top 10 most expensive resources in the subscription
az cost management query \
  --type Usage \
  --time-period '{"from":"2024-01-01","to":"2024-01-31"}' \
  --dataset '{"granularity":"None","aggregation":{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}},"grouping":[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ResourceId"}]}' \
  --output table
Subscription Lifecycle: Creating, Canceling, and Migrating

Subscriptions have a lifecycle: they are created under a billing account, can be renamed or moved between management groups, and can be canceled or deleted. Understanding the lifecycle prevents accidental data loss.
Creating:

new subscriptions are created through the Azure portal (Subscriptions → Add) or via the Azure CLI / REST API under a specific billing account and enrollment account. The subscription immediately inherits policy and RBAC from its parent management group.
Renaming:

the subscription display name (az account subscription rename) can be changed at any time. The subscription ID (a UUID) never changes — it is the stable identifier used in resource IDs, policy assignments, and role assignments.
Canceling:

canceling a subscription stops billing. Resources are retained for 90 days in a disabled state before automatic deletion. During the 90-day window, the subscription can be reactivated and resources recovered.
Deleting:
after the 90-day retention window, the subscription and all its resources are permanently deleted. This cannot be undone.
Transferring:

a subscription can be transferred to a different billing account (e.g., from EA to MCA) or to a different Azure AD tenant. Transferring tenants affects resource access because existing RBAC assignments become orphaned (the user identities from the old AAD tenant no longer exist in the new tenant).
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans at Subscription Scope

Azure Reserved Instances (RIs) and Azure Savings Plans are billing constructs that provide discounted rates in exchange for a commitment to use a specific amount of compute over 1 or 3 years. Both are purchased at the subscription scope (or at the management group / billing account scope for shared reservations) and apply only to resources in the covered subscription(s).
Feature
Reserved Instances (RIs)
Azure Savings Plans
What it covers
Specific VM SKU + region + OS + tenancy
Any compute across VMs, App Service, Container Instances
Flexibility
Lower — locked to specific SKU/region (Instance Size Flexibility helps within a family)
Higher — applies to any eligible compute automatically
Discount vs PAYG
Up to 72% for 3-year Linux
Up to 65% for 3-year
Scope
Subscription or shared (billing account/MCA billing profile)
Subscription or shared
Best for
Stable, predictable workloads with known VM type
Mixed or evolving compute usage across multiple services

# View existing reservations on a subscription
az reservations reservation list \
  --query "[].{name:name, sku:sku.name, scope:properties.appliedScopes[0], state:properties.provisioningState, expiry:properties.expiryDate}" \
  --output table

# Check reservation utilization (how much of the reserved capacity is actually being used)
az consumption reservation detail list \
  --reservation-order-id "order-id-here" \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2024-01-31 \
  --query "[].{date:usageDate, used:usedHours, reserved:reservedHours, utilizationPct:totalReservedQuantity}" \
  --output table

# Check Savings Plan utilization
az billing savings-plan list \
  --query "[].{name:name, commitment:properties.commitment.amount, currency:properties.commitment.currencyCode, state:properties.provisioningState}" \
  --output table

Low reservation utilization (below 70%) means you are paying for reserved capacity that is not being consumed. Review utilization monthly and either rightsize the reservation (exchange to a smaller RI) or ensure your workloads are running consistently enough to justify the commitment.
Tags at the Subscription Level

Azure resource tags are name-value pairs applied to resources and resource groups for cost allocation, ownership tracking, and operational filtering. Tags do not apply natively to subscriptions themselves in the resource tagging model — subscriptions are in the billing hierarchy, not the resource hierarchy — but you can tag resources within a subscription uniformly using Azure Policy.

A common governance pattern: enforce that every resource group in a subscription must have an Owner, CostCenter, and Environment tag, and that the Environment tag value must be one of Production, Staging, Development. Azure Policy with the 'Require tag and its value' built-in effect enforces this at creation time.

# List all unique tags across all resource groups in a subscription
az group list --query "[].{name:name, tags:tags}" --output json |   python3 -c "
import json, sys
groups = json.load(sys.stdin)
all_keys = set()
for g in groups:
    if g['tags']:
        all_keys.update(g['tags'].keys())
print('Tag keys found across resource groups:')
for k in sorted(all_keys):
    print(f'  {k}')
"

# Assign a policy to require Owner tag on all resource groups
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-owner-tag" \
  --display-name "Require Owner tag on resource groups" \
  --policy "96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "Owner"}}'

# Find all resources missing the CostCenter tag
az resource list \
  --query "[?tags.CostCenter==null].{name:name, type:type, rg:resourceGroup}" \
  --output table

# Add a tag to all untagged resource groups
for RG in $(az group list --query "[?tags.CostCenter==null].name" -o tsv); do
  az group update --name "$RG" --set tags.CostCenter="Unknown"
done
Service Principal and Managed Identity Access at Subscription Scope

Automating subscription management — creating resource groups, deploying resources, managing role assignments — requires a service principal or managed identity with appropriate permissions at the subscription scope. Understanding the minimum required roles prevents over-privileged automation accounts.
Task
Minimum required role
Notes
Read resources in subscription
Reader
Cannot create or modify anything
Create and manage resource groups and resources
Contributor
Cannot assign roles or manage policy
Assign RBAC roles to other principals
User Access Administrator
Can assign roles but cannot create resources
Full subscription control
Owner
Owner = Contributor + User Access Administrator
Create new subscriptions under billing account
Enrollment Account Owner (EA) or Billing Account Contributor (MCA)
Subscription creation is a billing-tier operation
Manage Azure Policy assignments
Resource Policy Contributor
Can create/manage policy assignments without being Owner

# Create a service principal for subscription-level automation
# (Creates an app registration + service principal with Contributor on the subscription)
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name "myAutomationSP" \
  --role Contributor \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --output jsonc

# Restrict to only specific resource groups (least privilege)
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name "myRGAutomationSP" \
  --role Contributor \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/web-rg" "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/data-rg"

# Use a managed identity instead of service principal where possible
# (Managed identity for an Azure VM — no credential to manage)
az vm identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --identities [system]

# Grant the managed identity Contributor on the subscription
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm show --resource-group myRG --name myVM \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)"
Subscription Vending: Automated Subscription Creation

In large organizations with many teams, creating new subscriptions manually via the portal is slow and inconsistent. Subscription vending is a pattern where subscription creation is automated via an internal portal or pipeline — a team requests a new subscription, the request is approved, and the infrastructure code automatically creates the subscription, places it in the correct management group, applies baseline policies and RBAC, and notifies the requestor.
The Azure implementation uses:
A Bicep or Terraform module that calls the Microsoft.Subscription/aliases REST API to create the subscription under the correct enrollment account or billing profile.
A GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps pipeline that triggers when a new subscription request is merged to a configuration repository.
Management group placement and policy assignment are applied immediately after creation by the same pipeline.
The requestor (team lead or architect) receives the subscription ID and management group path via an automated notification.

# Create a subscription via CLI (requires billing account access)
# Microsoft Customer Agreement example
az billing account list --query "[].name" -o tsv  # get billing account name
az billing profile list --account-name "BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID" --query "[].name" -o tsv  # get billing profile

# Create subscription alias (idempotent operation)
az rest --method PUT \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/aliases/myNewSubscription?api-version=2021-10-01" \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "displayName": "MyApp-Production",
      "workload": "Production",
      "billingScope": "/billingAccounts/BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID/billingProfiles/BILLING_PROFILE_ID/invoiceSections/INVOICE_SECTION_ID"
    }
  }'

# Check alias creation status
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/aliases/myNewSubscription?api-version=2021-10-01" \
  --query "{status:properties.provisioningState, subscriptionId:properties.subscriptionId}"

# Move newly created subscription to the correct management group
NEW_SUB_ID=$(az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/aliases/myNewSubscription?api-version=2021-10-01" \
  --query "properties.subscriptionId" -o tsv)

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name "Corp-Prod" \
  --subscription "$NEW_SUB_ID"
Diagnosing Subscription-Level Issues
Several common operational problems manifest at the subscription level. Knowing where to look for each saves diagnostic time:
Symptom
Likely cause
Where to check
Resource creation fails with QuotaExceeded
vCPU or resource-type quota exhausted in this region
az vm list-usage --location <region>; request increase via Azure Support
Policy compliance shows non-compliant resources
Policy assignment inherited from management group or applied at subscription
az policy assignment list; az policy state list --subscription
Principal cannot see resources despite Contributor role
Role assigned at wrong subscription or subject to management group deny assignment
az role assignment list --include-inherited; check management group deny assignments
Cost spike not tracked to a resource group
Resources tagged to wrong group or cost anomaly in shared PaaS services
Azure Cost Management → Cost by Resource; filter by subscription
Reservation underutilized
VMs in reservation scope not running or wrong SKU/region
az consumption reservation detail list for the order
New subscription created but missing baseline policies
Subscription vending didn't apply management group placement correctly
az account management-group show --expand; az policy assignment list at sub scope
Subscription Considerations for Compliance and Sovereignty

For regulated workloads (PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, GDPR), subscription boundaries can be used to enforce data residency and compliance requirements at the billing and access level, while management group policies enforce the technical controls:
Separate subscriptions for regulated workloads:

PCI DSS requires strong isolation of cardholder data environments (CDE). Running the CDE in its own subscription (separate from corporate IT workloads) prevents accidental exposure from shared resource groups and simplifies audit scoping — the PCI auditor only needs to review the CDE subscription.
Geographic subscriptions for data residency:

the EU GDPR and similar regulations require that data not leave specific geographic boundaries. A subscription dedicated to EU data, combined with Azure Policy restricting allowed locations to EU regions, creates a technical and administrative boundary that simplifies compliance documentation.
Defender for Cloud at subscription scope:

enabling Microsoft Defender for Cloud on a subscription automatically scans all resources in that subscription for security misconfigurations. For regulated subscriptions, this provides continuous compliance monitoring without manual auditing.

# Restrict a subscription to deploy only in EU regions (GDPR data residency)
az policy assignment create \
  --name "eu-regions-only" \
  --display-name "Deploy only in EU regions" \
  --policy "e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations": {"value": ["westeurope", "northeurope", "germanywestcentral", "swedencentral"]}}'

# Enable Defender for Cloud on the subscription
az security pricing create \
  --name VirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard

az security pricing create \
  --name SqlServers \
  --tier Standard

az security pricing create \
  --name StorageAccounts \
  --tier Standard

# View Defender for Cloud regulatory compliance status
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --query "[].{name:name, state:state, passedControls:passedControls, failedControls:failedControls}" \
  --output table

Subscription boundaries support compliance scoping but do not replace proper access controls, encryption, and application-level security. A subscription boundary tells an auditor 'this is the boundary of the regulated environment'; technical controls within the subscription determine whether that environment is actually secure.
Subscription vs Resource Group: Choosing the Right Isolation Level

A common design question is whether to isolate two environments or two applications using separate subscriptions or separate resource groups within the same subscription. The decision turns on which boundaries you actually need:
Use separate subscriptions when:

you need independent billing statements, independent quota pools (so one team's vCPU usage does not block another), separate compliance audit scope, or the ability to grant subscription-level Owner to a team without affecting other teams.
Use separate resource groups when:

the applications share the same billing account, quota pools are not a concern, you want RBAC at the resource group level (Contributor on just the web-rg, not the whole subscription), and lifecycle management (deleting the resource group deletes all resources in it atomically) is sufficient.

A practical rule: if two things would be harmful to manage together — different owners, different compliance requirements, different billing centers — put them in separate subscriptions. If they just need organized namespacing, separate resource groups are sufficient.

# Example: Check current resource group count in a subscription
az group list --query "length(@)" -o tsv

# Example: Create resource groups for environment separation within a subscription
az group create --name "myapp-prod-web" --location eastus --tags Environment=Production App=myapp Tier=web
az group create --name "myapp-prod-data" --location eastus --tags Environment=Production App=myapp Tier=data
az group create --name "myapp-nonprod-web" --location eastus --tags Environment=NonProd App=myapp Tier=web

# Grant a team Contributor on their resource groups only (not the whole subscription)
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee "dev-team@contoso.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-nonprod-web"

# List resource groups with their tags (useful for cost allocation audit)
az group list --query "[].{name:name, location:location, env:tags.Environment, app:tags.App}" --output table
Summary

An Azure subscription is the billing, access control, policy, and quota boundary for Azure resources. Every resource belongs to exactly one subscription, and subscriptions belong to a management group in the resource hierarchy.
Subscriptions generate separate Azure invoices; use multiple subscriptions to isolate billing by team, project, or environment.
RBAC at the subscription scope applies to all resource groups and resources within it; use management groups to apply roles across multiple subscriptions at once.
Service quotas (vCPUs, storage accounts, public IPs) are tracked per subscription per region; request increases before scaling events.
The subscription ID (UUID) is the stable identifier — it never changes. Display names are mutable.
Canceled subscriptions retain resources for 90 days before permanent deletion; reactivate within that window to recover.
